title: --security-opt label=role: inconsistency
body:
```
Version:      3.4.2
API Version:  3.4.2
Go Version:   go1.17.3
Built:        Thu Jan  1 01:00:00 1970
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

<!--
`--security-opt label=role:` is not consistent
-->

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**
```
podman run --privileged -ti --security-opt label=type:unprivcontainer.subj --security-opt label=user:wheel.id --security-opt label=role:wheel.role --security-opt label=level:s0 docker.io/library/debian:bullseye-slim
```
**Describe the results you received:**
```
Dec 11 16:54:18 brutus conmon[167588]: touch: cannot touch 'test': Permission denied
Dec 11 16:54:18 brutus audit[167608]: AVC avc:  denied  { create } for  pid=167608 comm="touch" name="test" scontext=wheel.id:wheel.role:unprivcontainer.subj:s0 tcontext=wheel.id:sys.role:containers.state.file:s0 tclass=file permissive=0
```
The file is not created with the specified role

**Describe the results you expected:**

File test with context `wheel.id;wheel.role:containers.state.file:s0` instead of it trying to create file test with context `wheel.id:sys.role:containers.state.file:s9`

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

SELinux roles can be used for container separation (Similar to MCS)

```
Version:      3.4.2
API Version:  3.4.2
Go Version:   go1.17.3
Built:        Thu Jan  1 01:00:00 1970
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

comments.body:
I read somewhere in the docs that "podman guarantees unique categories for separation" but I highly doubt that.

Say you have four users all running Podman, then how would these four Podman instances be aware of what sets of compartments they use?

That is where separation using roles comes in: In the event that two Podman user instance use the same sets of compartments, the instances will still be separated based on roles (if leveraged)
One should also ensure that if label=user: and label=role: is specified on the command line that the overlay is mounted with context= taking the user and role into account.

Correct if you had multiple users on the same machine at the same time, then there is a chance that the MCS Labels would conflict.

Podman mount label only supports a single label mount_file_t, specified in 
```
$ cat /usr/share/containers/selinux/contexts
process = "system_u:system_r:container_t:s0"
file = "system_u:object_r:container_file_t:s0"
ro_file="system_u:object_r:container_ro_file_t:s0"
kvm_process = "system_u:system_r:container_kvm_t:s0"
init_process = "system_u:system_r:container_init_t:s0"
engine_process = "system_u:system_r:container_engine_t:s0"
```
Separation on users would be handled by DAC.  Luckily it is very rare that multiple users would be running multiple containers on a single machine.
SELinux library does not handle file system roles and users at this point. PRs welcome.

> SELinux library does not handle file system roles and users at this point. PRs welcome.

Users and levels work fine, it's just roles that is lacking (at least for the `setfilecon()` part as the `context=` labeling currently only supports a single label I guess).

> Separation on users would be handled by DAC. Luckily it is very rare that multiple users would be running multiple containers on a single machine.

Exactly and that is also why I argued in another thread that Podman should rely on SELinux for defaults if no `lcx_contexts` exists (but still allow `security-opt label`). It is like you said: `lxc_contexts` only "supports single label" and that may have been suffice in the past but these day's with per-user instances it is just no longer helpful.

Anyway's luckily users can disable the label feature in `~/.config/containers/containers.conf`, and guess what. Ironically separation based on roles works fine them.

> Correct if you had multiple users on the same machine at the same time, then there is a chance that the MCS Labels would conflict.
> 
> Podman mount label only supports a single label mount_file_t, specified in
> 
> ```
> $ cat /usr/share/containers/selinux/contexts
> process = "system_u:system_r:container_t:s0"
> file = "system_u:object_r:container_file_t:s0"
> ro_file="system_u:object_r:container_ro_file_t:s0"
> kvm_process = "system_u:system_r:container_kvm_t:s0"
> init_process = "system_u:system_r:container_init_t:s0"
> engine_process = "system_u:system_r:container_engine_t:s0"
> ```

Yes there is no such thing as a "single label". You could at least have omitted the user role and level for `lxc_contexts`:

```
$ cat /usr/share/containers/selinux/contexts
process = "container_t"
file = "container_file_t"
ro_file="container_ro_file_t"
kvm_process = "container_kvm_t"
init_process = "container_init_t"
engine_process = "container_engine_t"
```
But aside from the fact that there is just no such thing as a single label, you would still get into `context=` mount label issues because ideally one should really be able to specify mount label user, role, type and level
Most of this conversation should be taking place in github.com/opencontainers/selinux

Two more observations:

Cannot manually override:

* pod infra container context (uses process =)
* volume (create) context (uses file =)
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@rhatdan What's going on here? Can we close?
Yes this should be working now.  Reopen if I am mistaken.
